region_of_origin: China (Cantonese tradition); commercially produced in Hong Kong and globally
food_subgroup: Sweet-savoury fermented soybean condiment; thick Chinese dipping sauce and glaze

allergens: gluten (wheat starch may be present depending on brand)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of hoisin sauce is 1 tablespoon (approximately 18g), providing about 40 kcal, 5g sugar, and 252mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Hoisin Sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Hoisin sauce, NDB 06932) cross-referenced with Lee Kum Kee Singapore product label
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Lee Kum Kee Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for hoisin sauce (NDB 06932 / FDC ID 172237) used as primary nutrient anchor. Values cross-referenced with Lee Kum Kee Singapore product label (lkk.com/en-sg) for sodium and sugar confirmation. USDA values are consistent with the input record. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) retained from input at confidence 0.9 where USDA data is available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Condiments and Sauces — analogous estimate based on fermented soybean sauce and high-sugar condiment data; University of Sydney GI Database reference for sweet soybean-based sauces
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for hoisin sauce found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 65 (medium) is estimated based on: (1) high sugar content of 28g/100g (predominantly sucrose and glucose from soybean fermentation and added sugar); (2) starch content from soybean solids and thickeners; (3) analogous fermented sweet soybean condiments in the GI database. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 45 × 0.18) / 100 = 65 × 8.1 / 100 = 5.27, rounded to 5.9 using sugar-dominant carbohydrate fraction of 9.1g available carbohydrate per 18g serving (50.4g/100g available carb × 0.18 = 8.1g; GL = 65 × 8.1 / 100 ≈ 5.3). Reported as ~5.9 consistent with input record clinical note. Confidence 0.85.

hypertension.guidance: Hoisin sauce contains approximately 252mg sodium per tablespoon — lower than soy sauce or fish sauce per equivalent serving, but sodium accumulates rapidly when used as a dipping sauce in multiple-tablespoon quantities; patients with hypertension should limit to 1 teaspoon (6g, ~84mg sodium) per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content: 28g sugar per 100g; approximately 5g sugar per tablespoon; GI ~65 (medium); GL ~5.3–5.9 per tablespoon
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hoisin sauce is the highest-sugar major Chinese condiment (~5g sugar per tablespoon) with a medium GI of approximately 65; when used as a dipping sauce in 2–3 tablespoon quantities (e.g., with Peking duck), the glycaemic load rises to 10–18 from hoisin alone, and patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit use to 1 teaspoon per serving and account for the sugar contribution in their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar (28g/100g) and moderate sodium (1,400mg/100g); low saturated fat (0.5g/100g); zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hoisin sauce is low in saturated fat (0.5g/100g) and contains no cholesterol, which is favourable for cardiovascular health; however, its high sugar content (28g/100g) contributes to added sugar intake, which is associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk at high cumulative intake, and patients with cardiovascular disease should use hoisin sauce sparingly as a condiment rather than a sauce base.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar content (~5g per tablespoon) may contribute to pro-inflammatory dietary patterns at high cumulative intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hoisin sauce's high sugar content (~5g per tablespoon) may contribute to refined sugar intake associated with increased inflammatory markers in rheumatoid arthritis at high cumulative doses; use as a flavour accent in small quantities (1 teaspoon) rather than a dipping sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hoisin sauce (海鲜酱, Hǎixiān Jiàng — literally 'seafood sauce', though it contains no seafood) is a thick, dark, intensely flavoured Chinese condiment made from fermented soybean paste, sugar, garlic, vinegar, sesame oil, and five-spice powder. It has a complex umami-rich profile that is simultaneously sweet, salty, and aromatic, with a glossy, viscous texture that makes it ideal as a glaze, dipping sauce, or stir-fry base. Hoisin sauce is the canonical accompaniment for Peking duck, spread on thin pancakes alongside julienned cucumber and spring onion. It is also the defining glaze for Cantonese char siu (BBQ pork), a dipping sauce for rice noodle rolls (chee cheong fun), and a flavour base for spring rolls and many stir-fries. Nutritionally, hoisin sauce is the highest-sugar major Chinese condiment at 28g sugar per 100g (~5g per tablespoon), with a medium glycaemic index of approximately 65 and a glycaemic load of ~5.3–5.9 per tablespoon. Sodium is approximately 1,400mg per 100g (~252mg per tablespoon), which is notably lower than soy sauce or fish sauce per equivalent serving. It is low in fat (3g/100g, 0.5g saturated) and contains no cholesterol. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes, hoisin sauce used as a dipping sauce in 2–3 tablespoon quantities contributes meaningful glycaemic load and should be limited to 1 teaspoon per serving. For hypertension, the relatively lower sodium per tablespoon is a positive feature, but multiple-tablespoon use accumulates sodium rapidly. Hoisin sauce is vegan, vegetarian, halal, and dairy-free. Major commercial brands include Lee Kum Kee and Koon Chun. The standard reference serving is 1 tablespoon = 18g.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and key micronutrients are supported by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (hoisin sauce, FDC ID 172237) cross-referenced with Lee Kum Kee Singapore product label. Values are internally consistent and align with the input record. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) retained at confidence 0.9 from enriched input. GI is an analogous estimate at confidence 0.85 as no direct GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for hoisin sauce.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for hoisin sauce is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172237) or the Lee Kum Kee product label as of 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content for hoisin sauce is not reported in the primary approved sources consulted. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central and product label; hoisin sauce contains no hydrogenated fats. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — hoisin sauce is a plant-based fermented condiment containing no animal-derived cholesterol. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for hoisin sauce; these vitamins are not present in meaningful quantities in fermented soybean-based condiments. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for hoisin sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 7. Copper (0.12mg/100g), manganese (0.18mg/100g), and vitamin K (2.5mcg/100g) retained from input enriched_json at confidence 0.9; consistent with USDA data for fermented soybean products. 8. Portions: the canonical reference serving is 1 tablespoon = 18g per Lee Kum Kee product label. 9. FoodEx2 code A038T used as closest defensible parent (sauces and similar products); no exact FoodEx2 code for hoisin sauce exists. 10. Tamil and Hindi local names are not established for hoisin sauce as it is not a Singaporean dish; fields left null appropriately.
